MTN crest sketch?

**Show, don’t summarize:** include dialogue snippets, code or classroom moments, design sketches.

## 	**What I Learned from Being a Writer**

When I was 18, I had a revelation. I was studying engineering at the University of Colorado, after my parents had refused to pay the application fee to Berklee School of Music. I was playing in a garage band that would perform in our dorm’s open mic night three times a year. I took it seriously, spending hours each day writing songs and practicing guitar; I wanted to go pro. For them, it was a diversion, something fun. I used to lie in bed and think of ways out of the degree I didn’t want to do, the future I didn’t want to have. I was good at it, it came naturally, but I couldn’t imagine following in my father’s footsteps. Some nights I would cry. And then one night, I had a realization: I would change majors to English; I would write, and I would teach.

	I had wanted to write ever since I read Roberto Bolaño’s *The Savage Detectives.* Its colorful streetwise prose convinced me that the best way to live was to wander the world and write.   
(maybe some more, but don’t get off track)

I went to China over the summer of 2010\.

**Artifact**: a billion things. Online publications. Photocopy of Z Pub?

**Lessons:** Not gonna make money writing  
Doing something passionate is rewarding  
Won’t always be what you think/want

## **What I learned from starting a short story site**

**Thousandonestories**   
**Wordpress—**Sometimes existing solutions are good.

Not only did I edit and write for the site; I did many of the illustrations as well.

I was in my senior year college

Frustrated with honors thesis and wanting to share my writing with people  
And thought, I can help others out as well.

So I started a WordPress site.

By the end, it had over 100? stories.

**Artifact**: screenshot

## **What I learned from Engineering** 

	Around that time, I talked to a recruiter at NetApp about my experience in China over the summer. We hit it off, and when I applied for the internship at NetApp I got it. **I had interned at HP** after my junior year of high school, but here I got to see the internals of a Fortune 500 company. I worked on their Test Automation scripts; it was my first time using the programming knowledge I had from **making Flash games,** from learning Linux and working at HP to a larger organization.

In the end, I accepted that I needed to face reality and finish my engineering degree. I talked to my advisor and realized that I could finish the English degree and get an engineering minor in just an extra year of school.

I worked for NetApp for a year. Now I was using C++, a language that was unfamiliar to me, and an archaic version control system to write a cloud-based backup system. I was out of my depth. Meanwhile, I fell in love with a girl. After she left to go and study in Europe, I decided there was no reason for me to stay in Colorado; I upped sticks and bought a one-way ticket to China.

^ Need to condense all of this, unless I’m writing about my engineering knowledge.

How to make it funny/human?  
Dad? Finally said “I love you”? Anger?

My dad was an engineer. He never seemed to love it. Actually, he always seemed to hate it. When he rushed onto phone calls after dropping me off at the bus; the coworkers he rarely named and never told stories about;

I wrote a story about going off into the world of work. I set it in Siberia.

But then I realized that these people who never seemed to love working during the day; they had good lives outside of work. They went to breweries. They mountain biked. They bought houses.

Artifact: find engineering notebook / notes from NetApp / In Situ

## **What I learned from Teaching English in China / Living in China for a Year**

I found a job teaching English at Suzhou Foreign Language School. I fell in love with teaching. I would get home each evening and grade for an hour and figure out what I would do the next day, stream episodes of Stephen Colbert when I felt homesick. The next day, on the five minute walk to school I would listen to the Colbert theme song on my headphones to get ready. 

Taught me:   
The basics of teaching   
Lesson planning, grading  
Time management???  
Organizational skills??  
Interpersonal skills??

Not to teach James Joyce to ESL kids (screenshot of Advanced English site)

What I did:  
Use github pages for a class page  
Would I do it again?  
Yes, but Google Classroom exists now.

**Artifact**: College English site

## **STEAD (What I learned from Teaching…)**

**What I Learned in Five Years Teaching English, Math,**   
**Computer Science, Creative Writing, Film, Skiing…**

	It was 7am in November, and I had to turn on the generator.  
First, pull the cord hard, hear the motor turn on; then, turn the key on the electric panel to turn on the lights and the power for the heaters that would fill the tents with warmth. My papers would blow all around the tent.  
And then, at 8am, class would start.  
Kids huddled by the heaters.

In a year at STEAD I had gone through a retreat where we brought kids to the YMCA in the mountains; teaching class out of a Boys and Girls Club and packing away all the chairs and ViewBoards at the end of the day so it could become an after school club again. And I did this because I *believed in it.* I believed we were building a school that would be good.



Kiki hated me.  
It was the cool October night of the Fall Festival.  
When it was my turn to get pied in the face, she did it with an astonishing amount of anger.

After some strange wording on her essay, I accused her of using AI.  
Then, later, she showed me the edit history on her Google Docs. I could see that she had made incremental edits over the course of the week (meaning she had written it). (/and this was pre-ChatGPT.)  
“I’m so sorry,” I said. “Sincerely I am.”

It was the last day of school. I was waiting for the girls to get out of the bathroom so I could leave.

“You’re a good kid, Kiki,” I said.

Later I heard her repeating this moment to other kids. “I almost cried,” she said.

Sam V made me laugh every day.  
He barely came to class, barely did any work. When he did come to class, it was to show other kids truck videos on his phone.  
Made me laugh harder than any other kid  
Then did the work  
Walked the stage; I teared up.  
“Watching you walk across that stage was one of the proudest moments of my life,” I said, surprising myself.

Kyler and I had an issue in class; he told me he didn’t respect me, because "respect is earned."
After the “family meeting,” I brought it up, as kind of a joke. “You’ve earned it,” he said.

	When Peyton spoke I went out in the stairwell and finally cried. This was the most meaningful thing I’ve done in my life. We worked hard for four years to build this thing from scratch; and now, in a way, it was ending. It was time to pass it on to someone else.



Learned classroom management, lesson planning organization; but more importantly trust, respect, forgiveness. Humility (to rule is to be ruled)

Being “on”; staying calm under pressure   
   
**Artifact**: ??  a billion things also  
Black Tie Gala? Would have to get permission

## **What I learned from making a (shitty) videogame / Trying to make a videogame**

I always used to think of videogames in my head.

I loved Homestar Runner and Penny Arcade and wanted to start a site of my own. I started learning Flash.

I loved to draw.

I made a simple game about a robot/humanoid who escapes from a tank. I put my heart and soul and all my brainpower into it.

Then my hard drive got wiped when I installed Ubuntu.

It was the summer of 2017, and I was high.  
I started working on it during college. I had just learned to make an Android app, and I wanted to see what else I could make.

I found somewhere you could download json data for elevation for anywhere in the world. I thought: this sounds perfect. I downloaded it for Mt Fuji, thinking: what the hell? I wrote some Python code to turn it into triangles that Unity could load.

        Tomato man (I was living off tomatoes and ham), 

**Artifact**: screenshots/videos

## **What I learned from losing my mind**

(Not related to work but helps the rest make sense)  
\-Managing my stress  
\-Managing my mental health  
\-(Meditation)  
I learned patience. I learned grace.

I learned self-confidence and self-love.

I learned to love exercise again.

I learned to love and forgive myself.

**Artifact**: KUNC video
